content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660            322-5901           322-6441              322-6444

July 19, 1983


Douglas A. Ingold
Attorney at Law
District Counsel
Resort Improvement District No. 1
9126 Shelter Cove Road
Whitethorn, CA  94582

                                                Re:  Your Request for Written
                                                     Advice, Our No. A-83-152

Dear Mr. Ingold:

     Your letter asks several general questions, some of which
may involve issues under the law outside of the Political Reform
Act,1/ which is what this agency administers.

                                FACTS

     You are the attorney for Resort Improvement District No. 1
which provides services (electrical, water, sewer, fire
protection, recreation) for the small community of Shelter
Cove. You have asked whether any issues arise under the
Political Reform Act as to the following questions.

                                QUESTIONS

     1.  Is it proper for the District to hire a person who
is related to a member of the board of directors?  If it is
proper what restrictions, if any, should be placed on the
director?2/

_________________________

     1/  Government Code Sections 81000 - 91014.  All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise stated.

     2/  In a telephone conversation on July 18, 1983, you
advised me that what may actually occur is that the spouses of
two district employees (the district only has eight) may seek
election to the district's board and, as counsel for the
district, you are seeking advice on the potential problems which
might arise.

Douglas A. Ingold
July 19, 1983
Page 2


     2.  Is it proper to hire two persons (for example a
husband and wife) if one of the persons, though not
supervising the other, has access to information (personnel
files, closed sessions of the board) that the other does not
have access to?

     3.  If the situation described in question #2 is
permitted, would it be improper for the District to hire
persons under such circumstances?

                              ANALYSIS

     Questions 2 and 3 are beyond the purview of the Commission
as the issues raised therein are not within the scope of the
Political Reform Act. As to those questions, you may wish to
consult with the Department of Fair Employment and Housing3/
as to rules on nepotism.

     With respect to Question 1, we can respond generally only as
to the Political Reform Act's requirements, you may wish to
contact your county counsel or the Attorney General regarding
other possible implications.

     The Political Reform Act provides that a public official may
not make, participate in making or otherwise influence any
official decision in which he or she has a financial interest.
Section 87100. An official has a financial interest if the
decision will have a material financial effect on a business
entity in which the official has invested (Section 87103(a)), on
real property in which the official holds an interest (Section
87103(b)), on a source of income to the official (Section
87103(c)), or on a business entity in which the official is an
officer, partner, director, etc. (Section 87103(d)).

     The District is not a business entity (Section 82005);
consequently neither Section 87103(a) nor (d) apply. No real
property is involved in the hiring decision; therefore, 87103(b)
is inapplicable. That leaves only Section 87103(c) relating to
source of income. If the relative is not a member of the
official's immediate family (Section 82029), or if
the relative has provided at least $250 in income (Section 82030) to
the official (director) during the preceding 12 months the
relative would not be a source of income to the director and
disqualification would not be required. If the relative is the

_________________

     3/ The Department has offices in Sacramento and
San Francisco.

Douglas A. Ingold
July 19, 1983
Page 3


spouse of the director, then he or she would not have to dis-
qualify himself or herself from participating in the hiring
decision because the payments to the spouse will not constitute
income.

     In the case of a spouse being elected to the board of
directors, the salary of the employee spouse is not "income"
under Section 82030(b)(2).  Therefore, the director spouse is
not disqualified from participating in decisions affecting the
employee spouse's salary.4/  See the enclosed advice letter
No. A-83-001 to Ms. Barbara Jenke for further elaboration of the
issues.

     If you should have further questions, please do not hesitate
to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                              Sincerely,

                              [signature]
                              Robert E. Leigh
                              Counsel
                              Legal Division
REL:plh

_____________________
4/   Although this is the case under the Political Reform
Act, it may not be so under other provisions of law (see
Attorney General's Op. No. 82-203).

Douglas A. Ingold
ATTORNEY AT LAW
Office: 434 Maple Lane, Garberville, Calif.
Mailing Address: Star Route, Whitethorn, Calif. 95489
(707) 923-3391

June 22, 1983


Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Re: Resort Improvement District No. I

Dear Commission Employee:

I am the attorney for the above District which provides
services (electrical, water, sewer, fire protection, recreation)
for the small community of Shelter Cove. I am writing to
obtain your guidelines with regard to the following situations:

1. Is it proper for the District to hire a person who is
related to a member of the board of directors? If it is proper
what restrictions, if any, should be placed on the director?

2. Is it proper to hire two persons (for example a husband
and wife) if one of the persons, though not supervising the
other, has access to information (personnel files, closed sessions
of the board) that the other does not have access to?

3. If the situation described in question #2 is permitted,
would it be improper for the District to refuse hire persons
under such circumstances?

I would appreciate learning the position of the Commission
on the above questions.

Very truly yours,


Douglas A. Ingold

cc: Resort Improvement District No. I

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-5441 322-6444

June 27, 1983

Douglas A. Ingold
Attorney at Law
Star Route
Whitehorn, CA 95489
Re: A-83-152

Dear Mr. Ingold:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Robert E. Leidigh, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division

BAM:plh
Enclosure

sections.conclusion: Questions 2 and 3 are beyond the purview of the Commission as the issues raised therein are not within the scope of the Political Reform Act. With respect to Question 1, regarding hiring of relatives: If the relative is not a member of the official's immediate family, then unless the relative has provided at least $250 in income to the official during the preceding 12 months, disqualification would not be required. If the relative is the spouse of the director, then he or she would not have to disqualify himself or herself from participating in the hiring decision because the payments to the spouse will not constitute income. In the case of a spouse being elected to the board of directors, the salary of the employee spouse is not 'income' under Section 82030(b)(2), therefore the director spouse is not disqualified from participating in decisions affecting the employee spouse's salary.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a small improvement district may hire persons related to board members, and whether restrictions on disqualification apply when spouses of employees seek election to the board or when related employees have access to different information.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Questions 2 and 3 fall outside FPPC jurisdiction. For Question 1, under the Political Reform Act, a director spouse is not required to disqualify from hiring decisions affecting an employee spouse because spousal income does not constitute 'income' under Section 82030(b)(2); however, other state laws may impose different requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Complete FPPC response letter with clearly marked FACTS, QUESTIONS, and ANALYSIS sections. Document references enclosed advice letter A-83-001 and Government Code sections. Minor formatting inconsistencies in original document (spacing) do not affect extraction quality.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
Is it proper for the District to hire a person who 
is related to a member of the board of directors? 
If it is 
proper what restrictions, if any, should be placed on the 
director?21 
II Government Code Sections 81000 - 91014. 
All s~atutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise stated. 
11 
In a telephone conversation on July 18, 1983, you 
advised me that what may actually occur is that the spouses of 
two district employees (the district only has eight) may seek 
election to the district's board and, as counsel for the 
district, you are seeking advice on the potential problems which 
might arise. 


Douglas A. Ingold 
July 19, 1983  
2. 
Is it proper to hire two persons (for example a 
husband and wife) if one of the persons, though not 
supervising the other, has access to information (personnel 
files, closed sessions of the board) that the other does not 
have access to? 
3. 
If the situation described in question #2 is 
permitted, would it be improper for the District to hire 
persons under such circumstances?

CONCLUSION: Questions 2 and 3 are beyond the purview of the Commission as the issues raised therein are not within the scope of the Political Reform Act. With respect to Question 1, regarding hiring of relatives: If the relative is not a member of the official's immediate family, then unless the relative has provided at least $250 in income to the official during the preceding 12 months, disqualification would not be required. If the relative is the spouse of the director, then he or she would not have to disqualify himself or herself from participating in the hiring decision because the payments to the spouse will not constitute income. In the case of a spouse being elected to the board of directors, the salary of the employee spouse is not 'income' under Section 82030(b)(2), therefore the director spouse is not disqualified from participating in decisions affecting the employee spouse's salary.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to questions from counsel for Resort Improvement District No. 1 regarding whether hiring of relatives and board member spouses creates conflicts of interest under the Political Reform Act. The FPPC concludes that spousal hiring decisions do not trigger disqualification requirements under state law, though other laws may apply.
